coredata.dc:title: Research on performance of liquid cooling plate for topology optimization of lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Wenxia
Shao, Yujie
Tian, Yongjun
Xu, Chenglin
Li, Huanhuan
Yuan, Jifeng
Yi, Aibin
Wang, Yaping
Pei, Lei
Wang, Nan
Yuan, Chaochun
coredata.dc:description:
The design of liquid cooling plate structure is an important research direction for the liquid cooling system of lithium-ion batteries. In recent years, structural optimization techniques have been increasingly applied to the design of liquid cooling plates. Among them, topology optimization is a systematic method for obtaining the optimal material distribution, which can automatically form optimized cooling channels based on the objective function. This article uses a density model-based topology optimization method to calculate the channel structure of liquid cooling plates, analyzes the influence of initial model parameters, and then establishes a computational fluid dynamics simulation model to compare and analyze the performance differences between topological structures and rectangular liquid cooling plates in temperature distribution, velocity distribution, and inlet and outlet pressure drop. Finally, the accuracy of numerical calculation results is verified through an experimental platform. The comprehensive results show that the topology structure with initial values of q = 0.01,
θ
0
= 0.5 andRe = 400 has better performance. Compared to traditional rectangular structures, the maximum temperature of topological structures has decreased by 1.62 K, reducing the proportion by about 0.53 %, and the temperature difference has decreased by 0.55 K, reducing the proportion by about 11.55 %. The maximum discrepancy between simulation and experimental measurements remains below 2.4 K, with a relative error under 1 %, both falling within acceptable tolerance thresholds. These findings validate the precision and reliability of the numerical simulation model outcomes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Topology optimization
Liquid cooling plate
Experimental verification

The impact of initial value of volume factor
Performance analysis and experimental verification of topological liquid cooling plate
Processing and manufacturing of liquid cooling plate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Geometric model and design domain of topology optimization.

The result of topology optimization: (a) The change curve of the objective function; (b) Flow channel for different iterations; (c) The three-dimensional geometric flow channel; (d) Topology structure coolant plate.
Geometric model of single battery: (a) Topological structure, (b) Rectangular structure.
Analysis of grid independence: (a) Grid number independence analysis; (b) Topological grid structure.
The impact of penalty factor: (a) Velocity flow diagram with different q; (b) Temperature distribution with different q; (c) Objective functions with different q.

; (d) Material volume factor with different

.
The impact of initial value of volume factor: (a) Velocity flow diagram with different Re; (b) Temperature distribution with different Re; (c) Objective functions with different Re.

Performance analysis of topological structure liquid cooling plate: (a) Maximum temperature and temperature distribution of single battery; (b) Maximum temperature and temperature distribution of the liquid cooling plate; (c) Maximum velocity and velocity distribution of single battery; (d) Maximum pressure and pressure distribution of single battery; (e) Temperature rise curves of single battery and liquid cooling plate.

Topology liquid cooling plate test platform: (a) Schematic diagram of single battery experimental system; (b) Liquid cooling plate; (c) Liquid cooling plate acting on cell; (d) Experimental platform for liquid cooling plate cooling of single battery.
Comparison of temperature variation between single battery experiment and simulation.

row.entry:
Hyperbolic tangent projection points
γ
β
Material volume factor in fluid domain W

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Heat dissipation performance of single battery test steps.

row.entry: Adhere power battery to liquid cooling plate, charge the battery at a constant current and voltage of 0.5 C at ambient temperature until the cut-off voltage is 4.2 V, the cut-off current is 0.05 C, and let it stand for 60 min.
Write the discharge program in the battery charge and discharge test system, start the micro pump and start the temperature sensor at the beginning of battery discharge.
The battery discharges the single battery with 1 C and 2 C discharge current respectively to the cut-off voltage of 2.75 V, and the discharge gap at different rates is left for 60 min.
The topology structure and parallel rectangular structure are respectively used to discharge and cool the single battery, and the relevant data are recorded every second in real time.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Error values of experiment and simulation.

row.entry: Comparison of simulation and experimental values
Maximum temperature difference at 1 C rate
Variance of error at 1 C rate
Maximum temperature difference at 2 C rate
Variance of error at 2 C rate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Horizontal comparison of optimization effects with existing research.

head.ce:title: Research on performance of liquid cooling plate for topology optimization of lithium-ion battery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
The design of liquid cooling plate structure is an important research direction for the liquid cooling system of lithium-ion batteries. In recent years, structural optimization techniques have been increasingly applied to the design of liquid cooling plates. Among them, topology optimization is a systematic method for obtaining the optimal material distribution, which can automatically form optimized cooling channels based on the objective function. This article uses a density model-based topology optimization method to calculate the channel structure of liquid cooling plates, analyzes the influence of initial model parameters, and then establishes a computational fluid dynamics simulation model to compare and analyze the performance differences between topological structures and rectangular liquid cooling plates in temperature distribution, velocity distribution, and inlet and outlet pressure drop. Finally, the accuracy of numerical calculation results is verified through an experimental platform. The comprehensive results show that the topology structure with initial values of q = 0.01,
θ
0
= 0.5 andRe = 400 has better performance. Compared to traditional rectangular structures, the maximum temperature of topological structures has decreased by 1.62 K, reducing the proportion by about 0.53 %, and the temperature difference has decreased by 0.55 K, reducing the proportion by about 11.55 %. The maximum discrepancy between simulation and experimental measurements remains below 2.4 K, with a relative error under 1 %, both falling within acceptable tolerance thresholds. These findings validate the precision and reliability of the numerical simulation model outcomes.

ce:section.ce:para: For the liquid cooling system of power battery, the structural design of liquid cooling plate channel is an important research direction. In recent years, structural optimization technology is more and more used in the design of liquid cooling plate. The complete structure optimization process usually follows the logical order. According to the optimization logic, the structure optimization can be divided into topology optimization, shape optimization and size optimization [1], and each optimization method follows different optimization principles. These optimization approaches are strategically implemented across different phases of engineering design. During the theoretical design phase, topology optimization determines fundamental configurations meeting optimization objectives; In the actual design stage, based on the structure obtained by topology optimization, the conceptual configuration is improved and the specific size is improved through shape optimization and size optimization [2].

In recent years, many scholars at home and abroad have carried out a series of shape and size optimization of the liquid cooling plate structure based on their design experience. Bulut et al.[3] conducted a comprehensive analysis on how the flow rate and channel configuration in multi-channel serpentine cooling plates affect thermal peak and pressure drop, and evaluated the system cooling level through hydraulic loss, convective heat transfer coefficient, and peak temperature. Su et al.[4] conducted comprehensive analysis of thermal management system employing U-configuration flow channels, established a genetic programming model, and explored its influence on the cooling effect of the battery system according to the parameters such as coolant inlet temperature, cooling plate wall thickness and channel height. Amalesh et al.[5] studied the influence of different channel shapes on the cooling performance of cooling plates, and found that liquid cooling plate with serrated and circular groove channels had better cooling performance. Sheng et al.[6] developed a dual inlet and dual outlet serpentine channel liquid cooling plate to improve the problem of poor temperature distribution in battery modules. Zhu et al.[7] designed a liquid cooling plate with inclined rib channels and added grooves on the plate to improve the heat dissipation capacity and temperature uniformity of the liquid cooling system. Compared with traditional liquid cooling systems, the temperature peak was reduced by 0.74 ℃ and the pressure was reduced by 55.73 Pa, greatly improving the ability to regulate temperature. Zhang et al.[8] added rectangular fins to traditional liquid cooling channels and optimized the fin structure and size spacing through biomimetic knowledge. The results indicate that when the fin size and corresponding spacing size in each direction are equal, the overall performance of the liquid cooling plate is the best. After multi-objective optimization, the average temperature and pressure drop of the biomimetic finned liquid cooling plate were reduced by 4.61 % and 54.26 %, respectively. As evidenced by their experimental findings [9,10], the cooling performance of different liquid cooling plate structures based on empirical design has been improved to a certain extent.

With the continuous progress of structural design methods, from the perspective of design logic, more and more attention has been paid to the theoretical design of fluid channel using topology optimization technology. Topology optimization is a systematic method for obtaining the optimal material distribution. In topology optimization, it is not necessary to define the structure of the cooling channel in advance, and a new cooling channel can be automatically formed according to the objective function [11]. The research on structural topology optimization started from Bendsøe and Kikuchi [12], and its main implementation methods include homogenization method, variable density method and level set method.

The advancement of continuum structure topology optimization theory has driven corresponding research developments in flow topology optimization challenges. Borrvall et al.[13] completed the topology optimization design of fluid problems by introducing Brinkman penalty model, which laid the foundation for the topology calculation of subsequent fluid problems. Yoon et al.[14] improved the calculation method of topology optimization through SIMP variable density method, and combined the N-S equation with the deformation tensor coupled linear elastic equation for overall analysis to study the influence of topology optimization structure on heat dissipation capacity. Dede[15] using the topology optimization method and COMSOL Multiphysics software, the topology optimization structures of 2D and 3D heat conduction and heat convection were designed, and the heat dissipation performance of the optimized structure was simulated. Matsumori et al.[16] introduced density based topology optimization integral equations to optimize the flow channel structure of heat exchangers while maintaining a constant input power to improve heat transfer performance. Chen et al.[17] used topology optimization methods to design liquid cooling plates for cooling battery packs, and compared the numerical results with rectangular channel and serpentine channel cold plates. The results indicate that topological cold plates have better cooling performance and lower flow resistance. Wang et al.[18] used topology optimization methods to optimize various inlet and outlet configurations of the liquid cooling plate with outlet enthalpy as the objective. The optimized liquid cooling plate was arranged at a 3 C magnification, with a maximum temperature of 304.49 K. Saha et al.[19] presented a topology optimization method for the design of a two-input single-output liquid cooling plate with improved thermal performance. The optimum channel structure is determined in the two-dimensional domain. Then, by using the three-dimensional liquid cooling plate model, the optimal inlet position is determined through the case study, and the advantages of the liquid cooling plate with the topological structure compared with the traditional straight channel are verified.

At present, the research on the structure of liquid cooling plate for lithium-ion batteries in electric vehicles is mostly based on subjective experience, shape improvement or size optimization, which can not guarantee the best performance of the liquid cooling plate structure. Different from these methods, the topology optimization method is not affected by the experience level of scholars. It can not only obtain the topology structure that meets a series of objective functions, but also make up for the deficiency based on empirical design [20,21]

Therefore, the liquid cooling plate structure with better heat dissipation performance can be designed based on topology optimization method. Therefore, this study will calculate the three-dimensional geometry of the topologically optimized flow path and the corresponding liquid cooling plate structure based on the topological optimization method of the density model. The influence of some important parameters, such as penalty factor, material volume factor initial value and Reynolds number, is then analyzed, since the change of some initial value parameters affects the calculation results in some angles, thus affecting the calculated topology. Then, the performance of liquid cooling plate with topological structure is analyzed according to the single lithium ion battery, and a test platform is built for the physical verification of liquid cooling plate.

Structural design optimization can be divided into size optimization, shape optimization and topology optimization according to different optimization logic. According to different optimization levels, topology optimization belongs to theoretical optimization, while shape optimization and size optimization belong to post-processing optimization. The basic concept of topology optimization is to replace the structural optimization problem with the material interpolation distribution problem by introducing the material distribution function defined in the fixed design domain. This structure allows the expression of arbitrary shapes, so that the optimal configuration of various design problems involving different objectives can be flexibly derived, such as mechanics [22] and heat transfer [23]. Compared with other optimization methods, topology optimization design is relatively more flexible, can get a new structure to meet the target requirements, and has higher optimization efficiency. For different optimization problems, optimization objectives are widely used.

Topology optimization of liquid cooling channel system involves flow simulation and convective heat transfer. Guest [24] based on Brinkman penalty model and Darcy's law, an alternative interpolation model using Darcy Stokes flow is proposed, and the viscous force coefficient is inserted into the approximate model to control the flow. In Darcy Stokes model, the interpolation between solid and fluid is based on the porous flow assumption in variable density method.

In this paper, the moving asymptote method based on Brinkman penalty function and Darcy interpolation model is used for iterative calculation of topology optimization. The design domain is regarded as a porous media model with variable density, and the fluid and solid materials are represented by different density values. The actual battery heat dissipation geometry and design domain are shown in Fig. 1. The dark color is the power battery in the working process, the power battery studied in this article is ternary lithium-ion battery, and the light color is the cooling liquid cooling plate. The heat generated by the power battery is transferred to the solid domain of the liquid cooling plate through heat conduction, and then the solid domain is transferred to the fluid domain corresponding to the coolant through thermal convection. The fluid domain absorbs heat and is discharged from the body to realize the heat dissipation of the power battery. Because the topology optimization process usually needs hundreds of iterations to meet the target requirements, if the topology optimization is directly carried out on the three-dimensional model, the calculation amount is huge and the calculation cost is very expensive. Therefore, from the point of view of simplifying the calculation and saving the calculation time, the three-dimensional heat dissipation structure is projected and simplified into a two-dimensional design domain as shown in the figure. The main structural components include topology optimization design domain (solid domain and fluid domain), fluid inlet region, fluid outlet region and constant heat source.

As shown in Fig. 1, the inlet and outlet of the topological liquid cooling plate are located on the centerline of the cold plate, and γ is the design variable. The value of design variable γ is between 0 and 1, 0 represents solid domain (black), and 1 represents fluid domain (white). When γ= 0, the permeability of the density model in the design domain is 0, and its corresponding flow resistance is infinite, which hinders the passage of fluid and is a solid domain; When γ= 1, the permeability of the density model in the design domain is infinite, and its corresponding flow resistance is 0, which can make the fluid pass through, which is the fluid domain.
(1) Flow equation

Since the fluid in the power battery liquid cooling plate is stable and incompressible, its governing equation is [25]:
(1)
∇ ∙ u = 0
(2)
ρ ( u ∙ ∇ ) u = − ∇ p + μ
∇
2
u + F
Where u is the velocity, m/s; p is the pressure, Pa; ρ is the density, kg/m3; μ is the viscosity, Pa·s; F is the volumetric force, N, that is the resistance in the flow process of porous material density model. According to the Brinkman penalty model, the relationship between flow resistance and fluid velocity in porous media is shown in the equation:
(3)
F = − α u
Where α is the permeability of porous media, md, it is related to the design variable γ. The equation for calculating α using Darcy interpolation model is:
(4)
α ( γ ) =
α
max
q ( 1 − γ )
q + γ

In the formula, q is the punishment factor of Darcy interpolation model, and
α
max
is related to Reynolds number and Darcy number, as shown in formula (5).
(5)
α
max
= ( 1 +
1
Re
)
1
Da

For the convenience of calculation, Eqs. (1) and (2) are normalized in dimensionless form, and the dimensionless form is [26]:
(6)
∇ ′ ∙ u ′ = 0
(7)
ρ ( u ′ ∙ ∇ ′ ) u ′ = − ∇ ′ p ′ +
1
Re
∇
′ 2
u ′ − ( 1 +
1
Re
)
1
Da
q ( 1 − γ )
q + γ
u ′
(8)
∇ ′ = d ∇ ,

u
U
,

p − p 0
ρ

2
, Re =
ρ Ud
μ
Where, d and U are characteristic length(m) and velocity(m/s),
p 0
is the reference pressure, Pa.
(2) Energy control equation

The heat conduction equation of solid is:
(9)
k
s
∇
2
T + Q = 0

The thermal convection equation of the fluid is:
(10)
ρ
C
p
( u ∙ ∇ ) T =
k
f
∇
2
T
Where,
C
p
is the specific heat capacity of the fluid, kg/m3;
k
f
is the thermal conductivity of the fluid, W/(m·K);
k
s
is the thermal conductivity of the solid; Q is the calorific value of the solid domain, W/m2.

According to the flow equation, the combined energy equation obtained by linear interpolation integration of the two regions is:
(11)
γ ρ
C
p
( u ∙ ∇ ) T = [ ( 1 − γ )
k
s
+ γ
k
f
]
∇
2
T + ( 1 − γ ) Q

When γ= 0, the unit is filled with solid, the energy equation corresponds to the heat conduction equation of solid, and Eq. (11) is equal to Eq. (9); When γ= 1, the unit is filled with fluid, and the energy equation corresponds to the thermal convection equation of the solid. Eq. (11) is equal to Eq. (10). Assuming that the calorific value is directly proportional to the temperature difference between the reference temperature
T
r
and the local temperature T, the equation for the calorific value of the solid domain is:
(12)
Q = H (
T
r
− T )
Where, H is the heat generation coefficient, W/(m2·K). Similarly, Eq. (11) is used to construct the dimensionless energy equation as:
(13)
γ RePr ( u ′ ∙ ∇ ′ ) T ′ = [ ( 1 − γ )
k
s
/
k
f
+ γ ] ∇ ′ T ′ + ( 1 − γ ) H ′ ( 1 − T ′ )
Where, Prandtl number Pr, dimensionless temperature T′ and dimensionless heat production coefficient H′ are defined as [26]:
(14)
T ′ =
T − Tc
Tr − Tc
,
H ′ =
H
d
2
k
f
, Pr =
μ c
k
f
Where Tc is the characteristic temperature, K.

The goal of topology optimization in this paper is to maximize the cooling effect of the liquid cooling plate on the power battery, that is, to maximize the heat exchange. The heat generated by the power battery is transferred to the solid domain of the liquid cooling plate through heat conduction. At the same time, the heat in the solid domain of the liquid cooling plate is transferred out of the body by the fluid domain through thermal convection, and the whole process realizes the heat dissipation of the power battery. In this process, the heat generated in the solid region of the liquid cooling plate is the result of the comprehensive heat dissipation of the power battery. The higher the heat generated in the solid domain, the greater the total heat value of heat conduction and convection, and the greater the total heat exchange. Therefore, we choose the total heat generated in the solid domain in the whole process as the objective function, and the dimensionless objective function is:
(15)
J =
∫
Ω
( 1 − γ ) H ′ ( 1 − T ′ ) d Ω

The mathematical model of topology optimization with fluid volume fraction as constraint is:
(16)
∫
Ω
γ d Ω ≤ WV
Where V is the area of the design domain and W is the volume fraction of the fluid domain. After setting the objective function and calculation domain constraints, the moving asymptote method (MMA) is used for iterative calculation based on the dimensionless energy equation.

For filtering, in order to avoid the checkerboard phenomenon (that is, the checkerboard like phenomenon occurs in the alternating dots between the solid domain and the fluid domain), the main reason is to deal with the problem of fluid solid coexistence. In this paper, the density filtering in the form of Helmholtz partial differential equation [27] is adopted, and its expression is:
(17)
−
r
2
∇
2
γ
̃
+
γ
̃
= γ
Where, r is the filtering radius, γ is the design variable before filtering, and
γ
̃
̃ is the design variable after filtering.

For fluid topology optimization design, the intermediate density region represents the solid-liquid coexistence zone between solids and fluids. Therefore, it is necessary to continue projecting the remaining solid-liquid coexistence zone after filtering to the 0/1 interval. To solve this problem, we set a projection threshold and use hyperbolic tangent projection to obtain a clear topology structure of the liquid cooling channel [28]. The projection expression is:
(18)
r
̅
=
( tanh ( β (
γ
̃
−
γ
β
) ) + tanh ( β
γ
β
) )
( tanh ( β ( 1 −
γ
β
) ) + tanh ( β
γ
β
) )

In the formula,
r
̅
is the design variable after projection,
γ
β
is the projection point, and β is the projection slope.

The initial values of each variable in the calculation model are shown in Table 1, where V is the design domain area. Since the size of the corresponding power battery is 149 × 28 × 98 (mm), the size of the design domain area is set to 149 × 98; Re is the Reynolds number, and due to the low-speed laminar flow during the heat dissipation process, the Reynolds value is taken as 400; Pr is the Prandtl coefficient, and since the cooling fluid is water, its value is taken as 6.78; H' is a dimensionless heat generation coefficient representing a constant heat source between the solid domain and the power battery, with a value of 100; In order to further reduce the proportion of fluid solid coexistence areas, the Helmholtz filtering radius is set to be the same as the minimum grid density unit, with a value of 0.001. In order to better project the fluid solid coexistence area to both sides of 0/1, the hyperbolic tangent projection slope is set to 8 and the projection point is set to 0.5. That is, when the design variable is greater than 0.5, it is the fluid domain, and when it is less than 0.5, it is the solid domain; The penalty factor value is set to 0.01, the Darcy number is set to 0.0001, and the material factor value for the fluid domain in the design domain is set to 0.5.

Fig. 2(a) illustrates the changes in the volume factor and objective function of fluid domain materials during the iterative calculation process. Fig. 2(b) depicts the velocity flow diagram of the simulation structure at different iteration numbers. When the iteration number is 20, observing the velocity distribution diagram reveals the presence of a large number of gray fuzzy areas of unknown solid fluids in the design domain; As the iteration progresses, the distribution of the velocity channel becomes increasingly clear. When the number of iterations is around 90, the channel structure has gradually taken shape, and the structure of the elliptical main channel and rectangular side channel gradually stabilizes; When the number of iterations exceeds 90, the trend of changes in the channel structure weakens, and the changes in the channel gradually stabilize. Although there are still a few gray areas in some branch channels when the number of iterations is 90, as the iteration progresses, the results obtained from the previous and subsequent iterations do not change much, and the change in the objective function value also tends to stabilize; So much so that when the number of iterations increases to 140 or more, some of the gray areas present in the above branch flow channels become clear, and the objective function value tends to be constant at this time, indicating that the number of iterations has met the target requirements. Even if the corresponding results continue to iterate, there is not much difference. Therefore, the number of iterations is taken as 150 to analyze the optimized structure obtained.

Due to the introduction of Helmholtz filtering and hyperbolic tangent projection methods in the calculation process to project and filter the flow channel structure, in the final topology optimization threshold result, the material volume factor less than 0.5 is the solid domain, and the material volume factor greater than 0.5 is the fluid domain (the flow channel in the figure is the fluid domain). Then, the threshold result is filtered to obtain a two-dimensional projection structure. Based on the filter, corresponding mesh parts are created, and after checking that there are no errors in the mesh parts, geometry is created based on the mesh. This results in a topology optimized two-dimensional flow channel structure file that can be exported and saved. The structure is exported as a DXF file and then imported into CATIA three-dimensional geometric modeling software to improve the two-dimensional structure in CATIA modeling software, Removing some incomplete flow channels and branch structures that expose a small part of the branches as shown in the figure, the final two-dimensional structural projection was obtained, and based on this, three-dimensional stretching was carried out to obtain the topologically optimized three-dimensional geometric flow channel and its corresponding liquid cooling plate structure diagram as shown in Fig. 2(c)(d).

Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) is an application technology that uses computers for auxiliary calculation. Its main content is to characterize, explain and quantify fluid transmission phenomena through numerical calculation of conservation equations [29–33]. This solution process is generally carried out on the computer, which can greatly save the huge cost of test equipment and human resources. CFD software generally has a powerful library of computational physical fields. For the simulation and calculation of the cooling performance of the power battery liquid cooling plate, it includes not only the separate heat generation condition of the power battery, the heat conduction condition between the power battery and the liquid cooling plate, but also the thermal convection condition between the solid domain and the fluid domain inside the liquid cooling plate. Therefore, this is a conjugate heat transfer condition with multi physical field coupling. In this paper, the multi physical field coupling software COMSOL multiphysics is selected for the simulation and calculation. Through the computer numerical simulation, the change and distribution of the basic physical quantities at various positions in the flow field under some complex conditions are obtained. The principle equation and working logic are as follows:

For pipe flow, the Reynolds number is calculated as follows：
(19)
R
e
=
ρ
c
u
c
d
μ
c

The mass conservation equation is as follows：
(20)
∂
ρ
c
∂ t
+ ∇ ∙ (
ρ
c
u
c
) = 0

The momentum conservation equation is as follows：
(21)
∂ (
ρ
c
u
c
)
∂ t
+ ∇ ∙ (
ρ
c
u
c
u
c
) = − ∇
p
c
+
μ
c
∇
2
u
c

The energy conservation equation for liquid flow is as follows：
(22)
ρ
c
C
pc
(
∂
T

Where, d is the diameter of the pipe section, m;
ρ
c
is the density of the coolant, kg/m3;
u
c
is the coolant flow rate, m/s;
p
c
refers to the coolant pressure, Pa;
μ
c
refers to the dynamic viscosity coefficient of the coolant, N·s/m2;
C
pc
is the specific heat capacity of the coolant, J/(kg·K);
T
c
refers to the temperature of the coolant, K; k refers to the thermal conductivity of the coolant, W/(m·K).

In this study, we simulated and analyzed the heat dissipation model of power battery. The corresponding geometric model is shown in Fig. 3. As shown in the figure, the size of the battery is 149 × 92 × 27 (mm), and the size of the cold plate is matched accordingly.

In order to compare and analyze the cooling performance of the topological liquid cooling plate, taking the traditional parallel rectangular liquid cooling plate structure as the benchmark, The internal channel thickness of the two liquid cooling plates is the same. The central channel width of the rectangular liquid cooling plate is 10 mm, and the branch channel width is 6.17 mm, four branch channels are dispersed on both sides of the center with a distance of 6.83 mm from each other. The specific structure is shown in Fig. 3(a) and Fig. 3(b). The liquid cooling plate is fitted with the largest side of the power battery, which can further enlarge the gap between the cooling effect while enhancing the cooling performance. In the model, water is selected as the coolant because it has efficient heat transfer characteristics; Aluminum alloy is used as cold plate material because of its excellent thermal conductivity and cost-effectiveness.

In order to reduce the amount of calculation and shorten the calculation time, the necessary assumptions are made for the conjugate heat transfer non isothermal flow model including heat conduction and heat convection conditions:
(1) The fluid is steady incompressible fully developed laminar flow;
(2) The fluid solid boundary is a non-slip boundary, ignoring the effect of gravity;
(3) The liquid cooling plate is homogeneous isotropic aluminum alloy material, and the material properties are independent of temperature;
(4) Without considering the positive and negative electrodes of the battery, the battery is simplified as an isotropic heat source with varying heat generating power.

The relevant thermophysical parameters of the simulation model are shown in Table 2. In model, the heat generation power of a battery is calculated based on the Bernardi formula using parameters such as the total internal resistance and entropy heat coefficient obtained through experiments[34], and the battery side interacts with the ambient air to exchange heat with a convective heat flux set at 5 W/(m2·K).

Since the results of the simulation analysis are highly related to the number of grids, in order to eliminate the influence of the grid number on the numerical calculation results of the heat transfer model, a grid independence analysis was carried out in this paper. In the grid independence study, five different gradients of the number of grid cells were selected to study the cooling performance of the single battery, which were 1.4 × 105, 2.4 × 105, 3.7 × 105, 7 × 105 and 14 × 105 respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 4, the maximum temperature difference of the battery between Gradient 7 and Gradient 14 remains within 0.1 K, meeting the grid independence requirement. Considering factors such as the amount of calculation and the calculation time comprehensively, the grid number gradient of the research model in this paper was selected as 7 × 105, and its corresponding grid structure is shown in Fig. 4(b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of factors influencing topology optimization
ce:section.ce:para: For the topology optimization calculation based on density model, the three-dimensional geometric flow channel of the topology optimization flow channel and its corresponding liquid cooling plate structure diagram have been obtained in the previous section. This section mainly analyzes the influence of some important parameters. The change of some initial value parameters will affect the calculation results from some angles, and then affect the calculated topology. In order to explore the influence of relevant initial value changes on the calculation results, this section studies the penalty factor, initial value of material volume factor, Reynolds number and other parameters.

In this section, the impact of penalty factor q is analyzed. When the value of q becomes larger and larger, the function interpolation curve gradually approaches to an inverse proportional straight line, and each γ interpolation range has a certain proportion. The penalty effect is poor, and the interpolation effect at both ends of 0/1 is poor; When the value of q gradually decreases, the function interpolation curve gradually approaches a power function curve close to the coordinate axis, and the penalty effect is good. Most of the γ interpolation is in the 0/1 range, and the convergence of the channel structure is clearer. In this section, three groups of data such as q= 0.01, q= 1 and q= 100 are selected to continue the research on the impact of the penalty factor. The rest of the topology optimization parameters are described in Table 1. The Reynolds number is 400 and the material volume factor is 0.5. The topology optimization results of different q are as follows：

It can be seen from Fig. 5(a) that when q= 0.01, the penalty effect of topology optimization is good, except for the upper and lower edge channels, there are still a large number of small branch channels. With the increase of the branch channel, the reaction area for thermal convection heat transfer is increased, and then the convective heat transfer between the solid domain and the fluid domain is increased; When q= 1, the penalty effect of topology optimization is relatively weakened, and the branch channel is reduced; When q= 100, there are almost no branch channels in the channel structure, only a few main roads for flow and heat dissipation, and the overall structure is close to the traditional rectangular structure. It can also be seen from Fig. 5(b) that the change of q value has an impact on the heat dissipation effect of the topology. The lighter the color of the area in the figure, the higher the yellow temperature, and the darker the color, the lower the black temperature. When q= 0.01, the proportion of dark color domain is the largest. With the increase of q, the proportion of light color high temperature domain gradually increases. When q= 100, the proportion of high temperature light color domain is the largest, the higher the average temperature of the whole structure, and the worse the heat dissipation effect.

The objective function obtained by topology optimization calculation for different q values is shown in Fig. 5(c), where the maximum objective function values of q= 0.01, q= 1 and q= 100 are 66.128, 51.918 and 50.295 (W/m2), respectively. It can be seen from the figure that the objective function value is gradually increasing with the iteration; The smaller the q value, the greater the final objective function value, that is, the greater the heat dissipation, and the better the cooling effect; Although q increases 100 times from 0.01 to 1 and 1–100, the objective function value of q= 0.01 is much larger than that of q= 1, and the objective function value of q= 1 and q= 100 is almost the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of initial value of volume factor
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, the influence of the initial value of the material volume factor
θ
0
is analyzed. The material volume factor represents the proportion of the fluid domain in the design domain. In this paper, the constraint value of the material volume factor is set to 0.5, but changing the initial value of the material volume factor at the beginning of the topology optimization calculation can change the time when the iterative calculation tends to be stable, and then shorten the calculation time. In this section, three groups of data such as
θ
0
= 0.5,
θ
0
= 0.6 and
θ
0
= 0.7 are selected to study the influence of the initial value of the material volume factor. The rest of the topology optimization parameters are described in Table 1. The Reynolds number is 400, and the penalty factor q is 0.01, and the topology optimization results of different
θ
0
are as follows：

It can be seen from Fig. 6(a) that the channel structures corresponding to different initial values have little difference. The overall roughly curvilinear channel is almost the same. The upper and lower edge channels are relatively wide, the middle channel is relatively thick near the inlet and outlet, and then gradually bifurcate into many small channels. There are differences only in a small number of branch channels. It can also be seen from Fig. 6(b) that the temperature distribution maps corresponding to different initial values are almost the same, and the dark and light color areas in each graph are not different.

Fig. 6(c) reflects the change process of the objective function with different initial values during the iteration, and Fig. 6(d) reflects the change process of the material volume factor with different initial values during the iteration. It can be seen from the figure that at the beginning of the iteration, in most cases, it tends to be stable faster, while
θ
0
= 0.7 tend to be stable for a relatively long time. From the change process of the material volume factor, it can be found that when θ 0 = 0.7, the volume factor decreases more slowly, which indicates that a high volume factor will lead to a lower objective function. The volume factors in all initial value cases will change rapidly to around 0.6 and then iteratively change slowly to around 0.5. Therefore, the final objective function values for different initial values do not differ much, and the change in the formed topological structure is small. However, there are still slight changes in the small flow channels, which can provide a reference for subsequent actual engineering manufacturing, reduce the processing difficulty, and eliminate the influence of the initial value of the volume factor. Among them, the maximum objective function values corresponding to
θ
0
= 0.4,
θ
0
= 0.5,
θ
0
= 0.6,
θ
0
= 0.7and
θ
0
= 0.8 are 65.768, 65.854, 66.128 65.987and 65.986 (W/m2), respectively.

This section analyzes the influence of Reynolds number Re. For a channel pipeline, after determining the fluid density, fluid viscosity coefficient, channel diameter and other parameters, the Reynolds number corresponds to the size of the fluid inlet flow rate. Therefore, this section studies three flow rate states of Re= 400 (corresponding to 0.02 m/s), Re = 1200 (corresponding to 0.06 m/s) and Re = 2000 (corresponding to 0.1 m/s). The remaining topology optimization parameters are described in Table 1, the penalty function q is 0.01, and the initial value of the target material volume factor is 0.6. The topology optimization results of different Reynolds numbers Re are as follows：

It can be seen from Fig. 7(a) that the Reynolds number is closely related to the number of branch channels. When the Reynolds number increases from 400 to 1200, the originally refined branch channels are further refined and the number of branch channels increases; When the Reynolds number increases to 2000, the refinement of the branch channel is further increased. From the temperature distribution diagram in Fig. 7(b), it can be seen that the darker the color, the lower the temperature, and the lighter the color, the higher the temperature. When the Reynolds number increases from 400 to 2000, the brightness of the design domain gradually decreases. It is the lightest at 400 and the darkest at 2000, which means that the average temperature is also decreasing. It can be seen that the increase of Reynolds number can effectively enhance the cooling effect.

The corresponding objective functions for different Reynolds numbers are shown in Fig. 7(c), where the maximum objective function values of Re= 400, Re = 1200 and Re = 2000 are 66.004, 86.584 and 92.196 respectively. It can be seen intuitively from the figure that the objective function value is the highest when Re = 2000, slightly higher thanRe = 1200, and the objective function value is the lowest when Re = 400. In addition, it can be seen from the figure that when the Reynolds number increases from 400 to 1200, the increase of the objective function value is much larger than the objective function value when the Reynolds number increases from 1200 to 2000. This phenomenon shows that the objective function value increases with the increase of Reynolds number, that is, the cooling effect increases with the increase of Reynolds number, but the enhancement effect decreases with the increase of Reynolds number.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance analysis and experimental verification of topological liquid cooling plate

ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the cooling effect of topology optimization structure and parallel rectangular structure with ambient temperature of 298.15 K and average flow rate of coolant inlet of 0.02 m/s are simulated and analyzed. The temperature of coolant and ambient temperature are the same, both of which are 298.15 K. The power battery discharges at a constant current rate of 2 C, and the discharge duration is 1800 s. The relevant simulation results are as follows.

The simulation results of the maximum temperature of the single battery are shown in Fig. 8(a), where left is the power battery cooled by the topology channel, and right is the power battery cooled by the parallel rectangular channel. It can be seen from the figure that the maximum temperature of the topology battery is 305.09 k and the minimum temperature is 300.43 k, and the maximum temperature of the parallel rectangular battery is 306.71 k and the minimum temperature is 301.4 k. Both channel structures meet the cooling requirements of the battery thermal management system, that is, the maximum temperature of the battery is in the range of 15 ℃ −40 ℃ and the maximum temperature difference of the battery module is not higher than 5 ℃. However, compared with the parallel rectangular structure, the maximum temperature of the topology structure is reduced by 1.62 K, and the reduction proportion is about 0.53 %. In addition, the cell temperature difference of the topology is 4.76 K, and the cell temperature difference of the parallel rectangular structure is 5.31 K. Compared with the parallel rectangular structure, the single temperature difference of the topology is reduced by 0.55 K, accounting for about 11.55 %.

It is not difficult to find from the observation of Fig. 8(a) that the temperature of the outlet is generally higher than that of the inlet because the cooling water will continuously absorb heat in the process of pipe flow and heat dissipation, so the battery temperature in the coolant inlet area is lower than that in the coolant outlet area. This phenomenon can be observed more vividly from the temperature change diagram of the liquid cooling plate shown in Fig. 8(b). The maximum temperature of the topological liquid cooling plate in the figure is 304.36 K, and the minimum temperature is the initial value of the inlet temperature of 298.15 K; The maximum temperature of the parallel rectangular liquid cooling plate is 306.2 K, and the minimum temperature is also the initial temperature of the inlet. Compared with the parallel rectangular liquid cooling plate, the maximum temperature of the topological liquid cooling plate is reduced by 1.84 K, and the proportion is reduced by about 0.60 %.

The simulation results of the coolant velocity and pressure of the battery system are shown in Fig. 8(c) and Fig. 8(d), where left is the power battery cooled by the topological channel, and right is the power battery cooled by the parallel rectangular channel. The color legend in the figure shows the distribution of coolant in the channel - the darker the color, the redder the flow rate of coolant, and the better the heat convection effect; The lighter and bluer the color, the smaller the flow rate of coolant, and the worse the heat convection effect. It can be seen from topology structure that after the fluid is input into the channel from the inlet, it is evenly distributed in the branches of each topology, and the velocity of each branch in the whole flow path is relatively high, which is uniformly shown as red and yellow with high velocity; It can be seen from rectangular structure that after the fluid is input into the flow channel from the inlet, the flow rate in the central main channel is the highest, and the diversion effect of other branches left to the main channel is not ideal, and the whole flow channel presents light blue with low flow rate.

The average flow rate of the topological channel is much higher than that of the parallel rectangular channel. In addition, it can also be seen from the figure that when the inlet velocity is 0.02 m/s, the maximum velocity of both channels has increased correspondingly, which is mainly caused by the convergence of channels. According to Bernoulli principle, in the flow field with constant height, the greater the velocity, the smaller the pressure. When applied here, the greater the flow rate increment, the more pressure will be reduced. Therefore, compared with the parallel rectangular channel, the pressure drop of the topological channel is smaller, which indicates that its energy consumption is smaller. In addition, it can be seen from the knowledge of fluid mechanics that the fluid will overcome the flow resistance caused by the loss along the way and local loss in the flow process, and the streamline of the topological channel has lower local loss than that of the rectangular channel, so its pressure drop and energy consumption are lower, which corresponds to the simulation result Fig. 8(d). The maximum pressure drop of the topological structure is 0.84 Pa, while the pressure drop of the rectangular structure is 1.05 Pa, which is greater than that of the topological structure.

As shown in Fig. 8(e) is the temperature rise curve of the topology and parallel rectangular structure. The geometric center of the largest side of the power battery is selected as the temperature probe point for data analysis. Among them, the temperature rise curve of the liquid-cooling plate represents the temperature change of the liquid-cooling plate at the center of the contact surface between the power battery and the liquid-cooling plate, which corresponds to the temperature map of the liquid-cooling plate in Fig. 8(b); the temperature rise curve of the single battery represents the temperature change at the center of the single battery on the side away from the liquid-cooling plate, which corresponds to the temperature map of the single battery in Fig. 8(c). It is more vividly observed from Fig. 8(e) that the temperature of the topological structure during the discharge process is always lower than that of the parallel rectangular structure, that is, the cooling effect of the topological structure is better than that of the traditional parallel rectangular structure for the single battery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Processing and manufacturing of liquid cooling plate
ce:section.ce:para: In order to compare and analyze the cooling performance of the two liquid cooling plate structures with the experimental platform, and to verify the results of the numerical calculation above, the relevant experimental system is established in this section. As shown in Fig. 9(a) is the schematic diagram of the experimental system in this paper. The experimental system theoretically includes battery testing system, high and low temperature test chamber, power battery, liquid cooling plate, micropump, low temperature thermostatic bath, water storage tank, and so on. Among them, the power battery is a 50Ah ternary battery. The temperature control accuracy of the high - and low - temperature test chamber and the low - temperature thermostat is ±0.5 K. The maximum flow rate of the micro - pump is 5 L/min, and the flow accuracy is within ±5 %. All parts cooperate with each other to form a complete experimental system. During the theoretical operation of the system, the power battery and the liquid cooling plate are fixed and placed in the high and low temperature test chamber. The power battery is discharged for 2 C through the battery test system. The low temperature constant temperature bath transmits the cooling water with constant temperature to the water storage bucket. The micro pump in the bucket drives the cooling water to cool the power battery through the liquid cooling plate circuit of the power battery, and then flows back to the water storage bucket. The real-time temperature of the power battery and the liquid cooling plate is measured based on the temperature sensor of the battery test system.

In this system, the processing cost of liquid cooling plate is the highest and the most important. Because aluminum alloy has excellent thermal conductivity and cost economy, aluminum alloy is selected as the manufacturing material of liquid cooling plate. Because the 3D printing technology is simple and safe, it can ensure the completion and safety of processing, so we choose 3D printing technology to process and manufacture aluminum alloy liquid cooling plate. It is worth mentioning that, based on the processing level of the current market, the cost of 3D printing technology is still relatively high, and it is not suitable for large-scale engineering applications. The actual liquid cooling plate obtained by 3D printing is shown in Fig. 9(b), and Fig. 9(c) is the liquid cooling plate acting on the single battery.

As shown in Fig. 9(d) is the actual working diagram of the liquid cooling plate cooling experiment. The micropump (power 8 W, lift 1.2 m) is placed at the bottom of the water storage tank, and the power battery is bonded and fixed with the liquid cooling plate. In order to achieve the experimental effect of the low-temperature constant temperature bath, the experimental area is kept in a constant temperature environment (293.15 K), and the water storage tank is stored in advance and placed in a constant temperature environment for 24 h so that the cooling water temperature is consistent with the ambient temperature. In the process of battery discharge, the cooling water driven by the micro pump flows through the liquid cooling plate circuit through the silicone hose and then returns to the water storage bucket to cool the power battery under discharge conditions.

The detailed experimental steps are shown in
Table 3 below：

Match the initial value conditions of the CFD calculation model with the experimental environment, and check the accuracy of the numerical simulation thermal model based on the experimental test data to ensure the accuracy of the calculation results. Due to the temperature fluctuation in the actual experimental environment, different initial values of simulation calculation should strictly correspond to the ambient temperature at that time. The results of simulation calculation and related experiments are shown in Fig. 10. It can be seen from the figure that the temperature rise curves of simulation and experiment are basically consistent.

For the topology structure under 1 C conditions, compared with experimental measurements, the maximum temperature difference calculated by simulation is 1.645 K, the relative error is 0.551 %, and the error variance throughout the entire discharge interval is 0.173 K. When running at 2 C, the maximum temperature difference between the two is 2.17 K, the relative error is 0.711 %, and the error variance throughout the discharge interval is 0.419 K. For the parallel rectangular structure, at 1 C, the maximum temperature difference between simulation calculation and experimental measurement is 2.181 K, the relative error is 0.732 %, and the error variance throughout the discharge interval is 0.271 K; at 2 C, the maximum temperature difference between the two is 2.322 K, the relative error is 0.763 %, and the error variance throughout the discharge interval is 0.338 K. It is found that the maximum temperature difference between simulation calculation and experimental data shall not exceed 2.4 K, The relative error ratio is less than 1 %, which is within a reasonable temperature difference range. Therefore, the accuracy and reliability of the numerical simulation calculation model are verified.

To further verify the optimization performance of the topological structure liquid-cooling plate proposed in this paper, we will conduct a comprehensive comparison with existing research on the structural optimization of liquid-cooling plates. Table 5 shows the general optimization directions and optimization effects adopted in the existing research.

Due to the different performance optimization focuses of different channel structures, we have chosen the core indicator of maximum temperature as the cooling performance for comparison. Through comparison, it can be found that the topology optimized liquid cooling plate structure proposed in this paper has good cooling performance and has a certain improvement in cooling performance compared to traditional structures.

Based on Brinkman penalty function and Darcy interpolation model, the topology optimization iteration calculation is carried out in this paper. According to the design goal, the correct objective function and its mathematical model equations are established. After setting the constraint equation and iteration termination conditions, the calculation is started, and then the topology optimization three-dimensional geometric channel and its corresponding liquid cooling plate structure diagram are obtained. In order to explore the influence of the changes in relevant initial values on the calculation results, the changes in three types of initial values, namely the penalty factor, the initial value of the material volume factor, and the Reynolds number, are studied. The results show that the topology performance with initial values of q= 0.01,
θ
0
= 0.5 and Re= 400 is relatively better.

Based on the energy equation, the CFD simulation model was established. Then, the cooling effect of the topology optimization structure and the parallel rectangular structure with the ambient temperature of 298.15 K and the average flow rate of coolant inlet of 0.02 m/s were simulated and calculated. The temperature of the coolant and the ambient temperature were the same as 298.15 K, and the power battery discharged at a constant current rate of 2 C for 1800 s. The calculation results showed that compared with the parallel rectangular structure, the maximum temperature of the topology structure decreased by 1.62 K, the proportion decreased by about 0.53 %, and the single temperature difference of the topology structure decreased by 0.55 K, the proportion decreased by about 11.55 %.

Finally, in order to compare and analyze the cooling performance of the two kinds of liquid cooling plate structures with the experimental platform, and to verify the results of numerical calculation, the liquid cooling plate is processed and the relevant experimental system is established in this section. The comparative analysis shows that the temperature rise curves of simulation and experiment are basically consistent. The maximum temperature difference between simulation and experimental data is no more than 2.4 K, and the relative error ratio is less than 1 %, both within the reasonable temperature difference range.

Yujie Shao: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenxia Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Chaochun Yuan: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Nan Wang: Supervision, Methodology. Lei Pei: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yaping Wang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Aibin Yi: Supervision, Project administration. Jifeng Yuan: Resources, Project administration. Huanhuan Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Chenglin Xu: Formal analysis. Yongjun Tian: Visualization, Software, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201426), Key project of the Department of Agricultural Equipment of Jiangsu University (Grant No. NZXB20210103), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.52172346), and Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund (JASTIF) (No.CX(24)3130).
